interpretedFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication and API Endpoints
interpretedFindings.description: Neither the login endpoint (/login) nor the API endpoint (/api/v3/attachments/...) enforce rate limiting. 15 rapid requests to each returned HTTP 200 with no rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After) and no 429 responses. The login endpoint is the higher-risk case, as it enables brute-force and credential stuffing attacks. The API endpoint risk is lower but could allow scraping or resource exhaustion.
interpretedFindings.impact: An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force passwords or perform credential stuffing against user accounts. The unprotected API endpoint may allow bulk data scraping or contribute to server resource exhaustion.
reproductionSteps: Send 15+ rapid POST requests to https://community.openproject.org/login with varying passwords for a known username.
Observe that all requests return HTTP 200 (or appropriate auth failure) with no 429 response and no rate-limit headers.
Repeat with GET requests to https://community.openproject.org/api/v3/attachments/120892/content and observe the same lack of throttling.
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Implement rate limiting at the application or reverse-proxy level. For the login endpoint, enforce a strict limit (e.g., 5–10 attempts per IP per minute) with exponential backoff or CAPTCHA after repeated failures. For API endpoints, apply a broader limit (e.g., 60–100 req/min per IP or API key). Use Rack::Attack (Rails), express-rate-limit (Node), or configure limits in nginx/Cloudflare. Return standard 429 responses with Retry-After headers.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# Example using Rack::Attack (Ruby/Rails)
Rack::Attack.throttle('login/ip', limit: 5, period: 60) do |req|
  req.ip if req.path == '/login' && req.post?
end

Rack::Attack.throttle('api/ip', limit: 100, period: 60) do |req|
  req.ip if req.path.start_with?('/api/')
end

Rack::Attack.throttled_responder = lambda do |req|
  [429, { 'Content-Type' => 'application/json', 'Retry-After' => '60' },
   [{ error: 'Too many requests. Please try again later.' }.to_json]]
end

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI) on External Scripts
interpretedFindings.owaspCategory: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
interpretedFindings.description: At least 5 external resources (scripts and likely stylesheets) loaded from cdn.aws-de-community.openproject.com lack integrity attributes. Without SRI, the browser cannot verify that the fetched resource has not been tampered with. If the CDN is compromised or subject to a supply-chain attack, malicious code would execute in users' browsers with full page privileges.
interpretedFindings.impact: A compromised CDN or man-in-the-middle attacker could inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes in the context of the application, potentially stealing session tokens, credentials, or performing actions on behalf of authenticated users.
reproductionSteps: Navigate to https://community.openproject.org/ and view page source.
Locate script tags referencing https://cdn.aws-de-community.openproject.com/assets/frontend/jquery.js and similar resources.
Confirm that none of these tags include an integrity="sha384-..." attribute.
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Generate SRI hashes for each external resource and add integrity and crossorigin attributes to the corresponding HTML tags. Since these assets appear to be self-hosted on your own CDN (cdn.aws-de-community.openproject.com), you have full control over the files and can generate hashes at build/deploy time. Automate hash generation in your CI/CD pipeline so hashes are updated whenever assets change.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
<!-- Generate hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary jquery.js | openssl base64 -A -->
<!-- Then add to your template: -->
<script
  src="https://cdn.aws-de-community.openproject.com/assets/frontend/jquery.js"
  integrity="sha384-<generated-hash-here>"
  crossorigin="anonymous">
</script>

<!-- For Angular build pipeline, use a post-build script: -->
# generate-sri.sh
for file in dist/assets/*.js dist/assets/*.css; do
  hash=$(openssl dgst -sha384 -binary "$file" | openssl base64 -A)
  echo "$file: sha384-$hash"
done

<!-- Or use the sri-hash webpack plugin / angular-sri-hash package -->

topIssues: Add rate limiting to the /login endpoint to prevent brute-force and credential stuffing attacks — this is the highest-priority fix given direct account takeover risk.
Add SRI integrity attributes to all externally loaded scripts and stylesheets to protect against CDN supply-chain compromise.
Extend rate limiting to sensitive API endpoints to prevent scraping and resource exhaustion.
